era_context: The Old Kingdom of Egypt (c. 2686–2181 BCE) continues under the Fourth Dynasty. Mesopotamia remains fragmented among city-states; the Akkadian Empire has not yet emerged. The Indus Valley Civilisation is in its Early Harappan phase. Bronze Age societies dominate the Eastern Mediterranean.

events.title: Reign of Khafre, Fourth Dynasty Egypt (conventional dating: c. 2558–2532 BCE)
events.description: Khafre (Chephren in Greek sources) rules as pharaoh of the Fourth Dynasty. He is the builder of the second-largest pyramid at Giza and the Great Sphinx. His reign represents the continuation of Old Kingdom monumental architecture and centralised power. Khafre's reign is conventionally dated to c. 2558–2532 BCE, but alternative chronologies place it differently. The assignment to 2741 BCE reflects one such alternative scheme.
key_figures: Menkaure – Successor (son or brother, relationship disputed)

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Khafre's pyramid and the Great Sphinx, Giza

events.certainty_note: Khafre's reign is well-attested in Egyptian sources and archaeology, but absolute dating remains contested. The assignment to 2741 BCE reflects an alternative chronological scheme, not mainstream consensus.
cross_references: Follows from reign of Khufu (c. 2589–2566 BCE conventional dating)

disconfirming_evidence: The conventional Egyptological dating of Khafre's reign (c. 2558–2532 BCE) differs significantly from the 2741 BCE assignment. This reflects the broader Egyptian chronology problem. No disconfirming evidence undermines the historical reality of Khafre or his monuments, but the absolute dating is contested.
historiographic_note: The historical record for this year is sparse and dependent on the same sources as -2740. The Egyptian chronology problem remains the primary source of uncertainty. The record is heavily biased toward royal monuments and court activities.

graph_edges.note: Khafre's reign precedes and leads to Menkaure's reign
